ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper presents a pragmatic, dataset-agnostic, rule-based pipeline for automatically annotating GEC edits (using a linguistically-enhanced alignment + POS/stem/dep-based rules) rather than trained classifiers. It clearly differentiates itself from prior work (Swanson & Yamangil 2012, Xue & Hwa 2014) by emphasising deterministic, training-free classification and transparency (“classify an edit based only on its automatic POS tags” and “entirely dataset independent and does not require labelled training data”). While useful and non-trivial in engineering and evaluation application (enabling per-error-type scoring on CoNLL-2014), the core ideas (edit extraction + rule classification) are incremental relative to existing alignment and classification research, so the novelty is moderate rather than highly original.

IMPACT.justification: The paper addresses an important practical gap in GEC evaluation by enabling automatic, dataset-agnostic annotation of system outputs with error types (rule-based classifier dependent only on automatic POS and linguistic markup) and by releasing the tool (‘‘we will release’’). Applying the method to the CoNLL-2014 outputs produced novel, actionable insights (e.g. per-team strengths on Missing/Unnecessary/Replacement edits, detailed determiner breakdowns, and detection vs correction analyses) that can guide system development and combination. Human evaluation reporting that predicted error types were rated Good/Acceptable in ≥95% of cases supports practical usability. This is likely to be widely adopted for evaluation and comparative analysis, opening follow-up work, though it is primarily an enabling evaluation contribution rather than a transformative modeling advance.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is generally well-organized and follows a logical flow (Introduction → Automatic Error Typing → CoNLL-2014 analysis) and clearly states its main contribution early (automatic, dataset‑agnostic rule-based annotation of edits). Notation and category labels (e.g. M:DET, R:ORTH) are consistent and the tables (Table 4, Table 9) and examples effectively support the claims. Presentation is marred by copy/editing and formatting issues (repeated sentences, broken hyphenation like “dataset in- dependent”, stray footnote/LaTeX artifacts and some duplicated paragraphs) which reduce polish but do not obscure the paper’s core exposition.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: This paper addresses a clearly NLP-focused problem (automatic error-type annotation and evaluation for Grammatical Error Correction) and proposes a practical, dataset-agnostic rule-based system that is evaluated on standard GEC data (CoNLL-2014) and via manual expert judgments (≥95% Good/Acceptable). The methods (linguistically-enhanced alignment, POS/dependency-based rules, comparison to M2 scorer and F0.5 metrics) and the audience (GEC and NLP evaluation researchers) match expectations for a top-tier NLP venue; it is well positioned within relevant prior work (Swanson & Yamangil, Felice et al., Rozovskaya et al.).

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper runs substantial experiments: automatic annotation applied to the full CoNLL-2014 system outputs with overall and per-error-type analyses (Tables 4, 5, detailed determiner breakdowns), detection vs. correction analysis, and a small manual evaluation of 200 edits showing ≈95% acceptable labels. Related work is cited and positioned (Swanson & Yamangil 2012; Xue & Hwa 2014; Felice et al. 2016). However, there are no ablation or sensitivity studies of the rule components or preprocessing choices, limited discussion of classifier failure modes (e.g. parser/POS errors) beyond surprising system results, and the paper omits the full rule set needed for exact reproduction (though tools used are listed and the authors state they will release the tool).

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper’s main claims (a dataset‑agnostic rule-based error-type annotator and its application to CoNLL‑2014) are supported by concrete system descriptions (Section 3.1: spaCy POS/lemma/dependency, Lancaster stemmer, Hunspell) and empirical application to the full CoNLL‑2014 outputs (Section 4, Table 4 and the per-team/error‑type analyses). The authors also report a small-scale human evaluation (200 edits, 5 annotators) with ≥95% judged “Good/Acceptable,” which provides direct validation of annotations. However, the evaluation lacks reported inter-annotator agreement/statistical tests and there is no analysis of sensitivity to upstream tagger/parser errors, which are plausible sources of systematic error for a rules-based approach; these omissions are minor but leave some questions about robustness.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper cites and discusses the relevant prior work (Swanson & Yamangil 2012; Xue & Hwa 2014; Felice et al. 2016; Dahlmeier & Ng 2012; Felice & Briscoe 2015; Rozovskaya et al. 2014, etc.) and applies its tool to the standard CoNLL-2014 system outputs, comparing against the M2 scorer and giving detailed per-system and per-error-type analyses (e.g. Table 4, operation- and type-level breakdowns). Comparisons are carried out on the same data and evaluation setup and the paper explains differences (rule-based, dataset-agnostic, transparent vs ML-based approaches) and provides a manual quality check of labels. However, it does not provide direct quantitative comparisons against earlier automatic error-typing/classification methods (e.g. reproducing Swanson & Yamangil or Xue & Hwa results) or trained classifiers on the same evaluation, which are notable omissions.
